metadata.summary: 6DoF VR design patterns: teleport-first locomotion, world-locked UI, seated mode, comfort settings, accessibility by disability category, 90 FPS motion-to-photon.

content.title: VR Design Patterns for Accessibility and Comfort
content.summary: Design patterns for fully-immersive 6DoF VR covering environment anchoring (ground plane, horizon, stable reference objects), locomotion menu (teleport default + smooth opt-in), UI placement (world-locked or body-locked, 1.5-3 m, eye-level, no head-lock), comfort settings (vignette, snap turn, FOV cap, seated mode), and accessibility parity across disability categories. Minimum 14-16 pt text, 90 FPS / 20 ms motion-to-photon, teleport-as-default.

why.p: Head-locked UI causes immediate nausea; smooth locomotion without teleport excludes wheelchair users and motion-sensitive users; no seated mode excludes a large population; text below 14 pt is unreadable even at 4K per eye. These are common mistakes that ship repeatedly because agents and developers default to web/mobile conventions. Teleport-first defaults and world-locked UI solve the most frequent failure modes.

list.item: Designing fully-immersive VR experiences (Quest 2/3/Pro, PSVR2, Valve Index, PICO 4, Vision Pro full-immersion mode).
Choosing locomotion and UI placement strategy.
Implementing comfort settings: vignette, snap turn, FOV cap, seated mode, height calibration.
Auditing an existing VR title for accessibility and motion-sickness risk.
AR/passthrough/MR — use `ar-design-patterns` and `immersive-design-principles` instead.
360-degree video in Cardboard-style viewers — constrained interaction, not 6DoF.
Mixed-reality productivity with passthrough — use `immersive-design-principles` blended-mode territory.
WebXR mobile sessions where 90 FPS is unreachable.

rule.statement: Keep the floor/ground plane visible at all times with consistent appearance. Never float the user in a void without a stable reference surface.
rule.rationale: Loss of ground reference causes immediate disorientation; the ground plane is the primary spatial anchor for the vestibular system in VR.

rule.statement: Maintain a stable, expected horizon position. The horizon provides orientation and reduces nausea independently of the ground plane.
rule.rationale: An unstable horizon forces the vestibular and visual systems into conflict, the primary mechanism of VR sickness.

rule.statement: Include at least one stable reference object (cockpit in vehicle sims, dashboard in driving, wrist-mounted UI) that moves with the user and is always visible.
rule.rationale: Fixed reference objects provide a visual rest point that decouples perceived self-motion from environment motion, reducing visual-vestibular mismatch.

rule.statement: Use 1:1 real-world scale for familiar objects (doors 2 m, tables 70-75 cm, chairs 45 cm, average human height). When using intentionally different scale, make the difference unambiguous and provide a human-scale reference object.
rule.rationale: Ambiguous scale breaks depth perception and comfortable interaction distance; users reach for objects at the wrong distance when scale cues are inconsistent.

rule.statement: Use consistent lighting that matches real-world expectations for time of day; keep light sources visible; ensure shadows enhance depth. Avoid extremes: too dark breaks visibility, too bright causes discomfort.
rule.rationale: Inconsistent lighting undermines depth perception and increases the cognitive load needed to understand the environment.

rule.statement: Implement a guardian/chaperone system: visual boundary at wall approach, controller haptic warning, audio alert option, experience pause on boundary crossing, easy re-centering action.
rule.rationale: Physical collisions with real-world objects cause injury; guardian systems are required by all major platform certification programmes.

rule.statement: Define minimum play space requirements at first launch; warn if the space is too small and offer a seated alternative automatically.
rule.rationale: Room-scale experiences designed without space constraints exclude users in smaller rooms and create safety hazards.

section.name: locomotion

rule.statement: Default to teleportation plus snap turn. Smooth locomotion and smooth turning are opt-in only, with vignette automatically enabled when the user activates them.
rule.rationale: Agents and developers default to smooth locomotion as the "modern" option; teleport must be pinned as default to protect motion-sensitive users and wheelchair users.

list.item: Teleportation (high comfort): best for general movement, seated users, and wheelchair users. Should be the default.
Walking/room-scale (high comfort for non-disabled): limited to the physical play area; excludes wheelchair users by design — always pair with teleport.
Controller/smooth movement (medium comfort): exploration use case; requires vignette and speed cap when enabled.
Vehicle/seat (high comfort): seated experiences; wheelchair-friendly.
Flying (low-medium comfort): specific use cases only; highest nausea risk; offer vignette and speed limits.

rule.statement: Always offer a seated mode that makes every interaction reachable without standing. Test with a wheelchair-user proxy before shipping.
rule.rationale: Seated mode is the most commonly skipped accessibility feature in room-scale VR; agents will omit it without an explicit check.

section.reference: Comfort-defaults schema with locomotion, turn, vignette, UI, accessibility, and performance fields

rule.statement: Place UI panels at 1.5-3 m from the user, at eye level (sitting or standing), curved to match the viewing angle. Never head-lock any UI element.
rule.rationale: Head-locked UI causes immediate nausea because it moves with every head motion; world-locked or body-locked UI stays stable relative to the environment or the user's body.

rule.statement: Minimum text size in VR is 14-16 pt; test readability at the actual 2 m viewing distance. Web/mobile point sizes do not transfer to VR.
rule.rationale: Text that reads fine on a monitor is unreadable in a VR headset at viewing distance; agents generate text sizes from web/mobile experience and must be corrected.

rule.statement: Prefer diegetic UI (holographic displays, wrist-mounted, screens on objects) where it fits the narrative. Reserve non-diegetic UI (pause menus, settings, HUDs) for meta-level interactions with clear visual distinction from the world.
rule.rationale: Mixed diegetic and non-diegetic without clear distinction breaks presence and increases cognitive load.

rule.statement: Implement gaze-plus-confirm selection (gaze highlights the object, button press or dwell confirms) as an accessible baseline interaction that works without hand precision.
rule.rationale: Gaze selection works from a distance, is accessible to users with reduced hand mobility, and requires no controller precision.

rule.statement: Confirm every button press with a spatial audio cue and synchronized haptic feedback. Add 3D-positioned audio cues for out-of-view events.
rule.rationale: Multimodal feedback confirms interactions for users who may not see or feel a single modality; spatial audio for off-screen events prevents missing critical signals.

list.item: High contrast mode toggle.
Large text options (minimum 14-16 pt; verify at actual VR viewing distance, not on monitor).
Color-blind safe palette as an option; test designs with deuteranopia, protanopia, and tritanopia simulations.
Audio descriptions for all informative visual-only elements.
Screen-reader integration is experimental in most VR platforms; document known gaps explicitly.

rule.statement: Add visual captions in 3D space for every audio-cued event. Caption size and position must be adjustable by the user.
rule.rationale: Spatial audio is the primary feedback channel in VR for many critical events; deaf and hard-of-hearing users need visual equivalents.

list.item: Haptic feedback synchronized with sounds.
Visual flash indicators for directional audio alerts from off-screen sources.
Per-source volume controls (separate sliders for music, SFX, voice, UI).

rule.statement: Every interaction in the experience must be reachable from a seated position. Explicitly test the interaction reach envelope for a seated player before shipping.
rule.rationale: Room-scale designs assume standing; seated-mode parity requires deliberate design of interaction distances and heights.

list.item: Teleportation available for all movement (not only as an afterthought comfort option).
Voice commands mapped to all primary actions (select, menu, pause, help).
Controller-free hand tracking as an optional input mode.
Adjustable height offset to accommodate different sitting heights and postures.
All content reachable within a standard wheelchair-accessible interaction arc.
Simplified UI mode with reduced information density and fewer simultaneous choices.
Clear, step-by-step tutorials; no assumed prior VR experience from first-time users.
Pauseable at any point with no time pressure during the paused state.
Difficulty adjustment where content difficulty varies.
Memory aids: contextual hints, progress indicators, reminders of the current objective.

rule.statement: Test with at least one person prone to motion sickness, one wheelchair user (or seated-only tester), and one first-time VR user before any public release.
rule.rationale: Developer familiarity with XR normalizes discomfort and novelty; the three tester profiles reveal distinct failure modes that internal teams miss.

rule.statement: Run at least one 30-minute extended-session test; short demos hide fatigue, drift, and cumulative discomfort that only appear over time.
rule.rationale: Comfort problems compound over time; a 10-minute demo that feels fine can produce nausea and arm fatigue at minute 25.

list.item: Default locomotion = teleport + snap turn; smooth options gated behind explicit user opt-in with FOV vignette on.
Always provide a seated mode that reaches every interaction — wheelchair-user parity, not afterthought.
Minimum 14–16 pt text in VR (smaller is unreadable even at 4K per eye); test at 2 m viewing distance.
World-lock or body-lock UI; never head-lock. Diegetic where it makes narrative sense.
Frame budget is non-negotiable: drop visual fidelity before dropping below 90 FPS.
Run hardware-IPD calibration step at first launch; a wrong IPD nukes comfort regardless of design.
Test with first-time VR users — experienced devs underestimate locomotion difficulty.
Agents recommend "smooth locomotion for immersion" as default — pin teleport-as-default in your prompt.
LLMs forget seated mode in room-scale designs; require explicit seated-parity check on every interaction.
Generated text sizes default to web/mobile pt — multiply by ~1.5x for VR readability.
Agents propose head-locked HUDs from gaming experience — explicitly forbid head-lock; require world or body lock.
Spatial-audio recommendations are often described but not specced — require Wwise/FMOD event names or equivalent.
Comfort-claim hallucinations ("reduces nausea by X%") — strip unsupported numbers.
Human-in-loop checkpoints: motion-sensitive tester pass, wheelchair-user parity check, IPD calibration QA, 30-min extended-session test.
Patterns assume 90 FPS @ <20 ms motion-to-photon — mobile/standalone may not hit that and recommendations break.
"Comfort" framings are population-level; ~5–10 % of users get sick on rated-comfortable experiences.
Hand tracking guidance lags devices — Quest 3 hand tracking is more capable than Quest 2 and patterns conflate them.
Screen-reader story is still experimental in VR — recommendations here are aspirational.
Multi-user social VR adds harassment + safety patterns this methodology does not cover.

template.purpose: Comfort-defaults JSON schema for VR (locomotion, turn, comfort, UI, accessibility, performance).
